summary: German pessimistic philosopher who wrote The World as Will and Representation, drawing on the Upanishads and Kant's thing-in-itself to argue that all reality is a manifestation of an irrational, amoral 'will to life.' Known for placing music at the top of an arts hierarchy, his compassion-based ethics in On the Basis of Morality, and his critiques of Kant and Hegel.

works.name: The Will and the Thing-in-Itself
works.description: The central idea of Schopenhauer's philosophy is that the 'will' (Wille zum Leben, or will-to-life) is the thing-in-itself, the fundamental reality underlying all phenomena. He identifies the will with Kant's thing-in-itself but rejects Kant's claim of a causal relationship between sensations and things-in-themselves, arguing they are aspects of the same reality. The will is a 'blind incessant impulse' that drives human experience, and the world of phenomena (including the human body) is the will 'objectified.' He uses the Australian bull-ant, which fights with itself after being severed in half, to illustrate the will's blind striving. Philipp Mainlander later argued only individual will exists, contra Schopenhauer's cosmic version.

clues.clue: Identifies the will / will-to-life with the Kantian thing-in-itself as the fundamental reality of the universe
examples: a book that also praises the Upanishads
claims all reality is the manifestation of an irrational, amoral 'will to life'

clues.clue: The world of phenomena is the will 'objectified'; Platonic forms arise from the will's objectification

advocated aestheticism and asceticism as avenues of escape from suffering

works.name: Aesthetics and Music
works.description: Schopenhauer's aesthetics place music at the top of a hierarchy of the arts, arguing it is the only art form that directly copies the will itself rather than merely imitating Platonic Ideas. He calls listening to music 'an unconscious exercise in metaphysics' and extends harmonies to the animal world and melodies to the human world. Architecture and landscaping are the lowest art forms. Aesthetic contemplation releases one from the will's blind striving, and the sublime bears a hostile relation to the will. Arthur Danto built on Schopenhauer's ideas, and Schopenhauer's aesthetics heavily influenced Nietzsche's Birth of Tragedy (Apollonian/Dionysian).

clues.clue: Music is the highest art form, the only one that directly copies the will / universal principle
examples: argued that music was the only artform that copies the universal principle that drives human experience
listening to music 'an unconscious exercise in metaphysics' since its structure reflects the true structure of the world

clues.clue: Hierarchy of arts: music at top; architecture and landscaping at bottom

examples: formulated an extended analogy where harmonies correspond to the animal world and melodies correspond to the human world

works.name: Critique of Kant
works.description: Schopenhauer engaged extensively with Kant's philosophy, praising Kant for showing how the perceiving mind separates the thing-in-itself from appearances, but criticizing many aspects. He rejected eleven of Kant's twelve categories as redundant (retaining only causality), attacked Kant's schemata, criticized proofs of Euclid's parallel postulate, and argued Kant failed to distinguish abstract from intuitive knowledge. His appendix criticizing Kant had a Voltaire quote attached. Neo-Kantians were particularly interested in his critique.

clues.clue: Praised Kant but criticized extensively; rejected 11 of 12 categories, retaining only causality
examples: rejected eleven of Kant's twelve categories as redundant, retaining only the category of causality

clues.clue: Critiqued Kant's schemata and method of symmetrical analogy
examples: critiqued the method of symmetrical analogy in his criticism of the schemata of Kant

clues.clue: Argued Kant failed to distinguish abstract knowledge of concepts from direct perceptual knowledge
examples: argued that Kant failed to distinguish between abstract knowledge of concepts and direct perceptual knowledge of objects

clues.clue: Disagreed with Kant's categorical imperative due to its egoistic nature; all moral imperatives are hypothetical, not categorical
examples: disagreed with Kant's assessment that human beings are driven to ethical actions thanks to a categorical imperative due to its egoistic nature
argued that all moral imperatives are hypothetical, not categorical

clues.clue: Neo-Kantians interested in his critique of Kant
examples: Neo-Kantians were particularly interested in the critique of Kant that this man offered

works.name: On the Basis of Morality (Compassion Ethics)
works.description: On the Basis of Morality is Schopenhauer's essay on ethics arguing that compassion (mitleid) is the foundation of morality and the only true non-egoistic moral motive. He argues all moral principles express the Latin dictum 'Neminem laede' (harm no one). The essay was the only submission to a Royal Danish Society contest but was rejected by an angry Hegelian. He also wrote On the Freedom of the Will for the Royal Norwegian Society of Sciences, outlining 'physical,' 'intellectual,' and 'moral' types of freedom and taking the compatibilist position 'man can do what he wills but cannot will what he wills.'

examples: declares mitleid, or compassion, to be the foundation of ethics

works.name: On the Fourfold Root of the Principle of Sufficient Reason
works.description: Schopenhauer's doctoral dissertation argues that the Leibnizian principle of sufficient reason (everything must have a cause) divides into four roots: becoming, knowing, being, and willing. These are the irreducible subject-object relations that explain all phenomena. He argued coexistence is only possible through the union of time and space.

clues.clue: Fourfold root: becoming, knowing, being, willing as four subject-object relations / explanations
examples: identified 'acting,' 'willing,' 'becoming' and 'knowing' as the four subject-object relations

clues.clue: 'First class of objects' cannot be represented without 'union of Time and Space'
examples: what he called the 'first class of objects for the subject' could not be represented without the 'union of Time and Space'

works.name: Parerga and Paralipomena and Other Works
works.description: Parerga and Paralipomena ('Appendices and Omissions') contains the hedgehog/porcupine dilemma (a metaphor for human intimacy), the 'Essay on Spirit-Seeing' (the 'dream organ'), the misogynistic essay 'On Women,' and the 'Metaphysics of Love' (praising homosexuality for preventing ill-begotten children). The Art of Being Right (38 Ways to Win an Argument) is a sarcastic guide to debate tactics. He also wrote a pamphlet on the humour theory (incongruity between concepts and perception).

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Schopenhauer is identified as a German pessimist, heavily influenced by the Upanishads and Indian thought (quoting 'tat tvam asi' from the Chandogya Upanishad), a student of Fichte whose lectures he found boring, and a bitter rival of Hegel. He scheduled his lectures at the same time as Hegel's but could never enroll enough students. Thomas Mann edited an abridged version of his magnum opus. Nietzsche praised him 'as Educator' in the Untimely Meditations, and his ideas about will and representation correspond closely to the Apollonian/Dionysian. He owned a succession of poodles all named Atman/Atma, and argued about the suffering of animals.

comprehensive_summary:
Arthur Schopenhauer is one of the most frequently clued philosophers in quizbowl, appearing with 20 tossups and 19 bonuses in the dataset. His universal giveaway is as the German pessimist who wrote The World as Will and Representation.

His central philosophical idea is that the 'will' (Wille zum Leben, will-to-life) is the thing-in-itself, the fundamental reality underlying all phenomena. He identifies it with Kant's thing-in-itself but rejects Kant's causal account, arguing sensations and things-in-themselves are aspects of the same reality. The entire phenomenal world, including the human body, is the will 'objectified.' Natural forces like gravity and electricity represent the will's lowest grade of objectification. He illustrates the will's blind striving with the Australian bull-ant that fights itself after being cut in half, and uses the Scholastic term 'principle of individuation' (principium individuationis) for space and time. Asceticism, denying the will-to-life, is the only escape from suffering.

His aesthetics place music at the top of a hierarchy of arts because it directly copies the will itself rather than imitating Platonic Ideas. He calls music 'an unconscious exercise in metaphysics.' Aesthetic contemplation releases one from the will. The sublime bears a hostile relation to the will, and tragedy draws out its highest degree. Richard Wagner changed the Ring Cycle ending after reading Schopenhauer.

On the Basis of Morality argues compassion (mitleid) is the only non-egoistic moral motive and the foundation of ethics. Despite being the only entry, it was rejected from a Royal Danish Society contest by an angry Hegelian. He also argues all moral imperatives are hypothetical, not categorical. On the Freedom of the Will takes the compatibilist position: 'man can do what he wills but cannot will what he wills.'

His doctoral dissertation, On the Fourfold Root of the Principle of Sufficient Reason, divides the principle into four roots: becoming, knowing, being, and willing. He critiqued Kant extensively, rejecting 11 of 12 categories and retaining only causality.

Parerga and Paralipomena contains the hedgehog dilemma (intimacy), the 'Essay on Spirit-Seeing' (dream organ), and the misogynistic 'On Women.' The Art of Being Right lists 38 sarcastic debate tactics.

Biographically, he drew heavily on the Upanishads (quoting 'tat tvam asi' from the Chandogya), was a student of Fichte, despised Hegel, owned poodles named Atman, and argued for animal rights. Nietzsche praised him 'as Educator' in the Untimely Meditations.
